content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 28,
1993
Jim Marino
Special Project Coordinator
DeWalt Corporation
2340 Niles Street
Bakersfield, California
93306
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-114
Dear Mr.
Marino:
You have requested advice regarding your duties as a planning
commissioner under the conflict—of—interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”) 1
QUESTIONS
1)
There is an application before the Bakersfield City
Council to amend the sphere of influence.
May you appear before
the city council in your capacity as an employee of the DeWalt
Corporation on behalf of your clients, the owners of McAllister
Ranch?
2)
May you appear before
LAFCO in your capacity as an
employee of the DeWalt corporation on behalf of your clients, the
owners of McAllister Ranch?
CONCLUS IONS
1 and
2)
Under the Act,
as long as you do not purport to
represent the planning commission,
you may represent your clients
in your private capacity before the city council and LAFCO.
FACTS
You serve as
a planning commissioner for the City of
Bakersfield.
You are employed as a special projects coordinator
for the DeWalt Corporation,
a civil engineering,
surveying,
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-93-114
Page
2
planning and land development company in Kern
County, California.
You are a salaried employee, with no ownership interest in the
company.
The DeWalt Corporation represents the owners of McAllister
Ranch,
a master planned community,
in the unincorporated area in
the county of Kern.
In the past twelve months,
your employer has
paid you approximately $20,000 for your work on the McAllister
Ranch project.
There is a possibility that you may acquire an
ownership interest in the project in the future.
An issue coming before the Bakersfield City Council regards
amendment to the current sphere of influence line, which will
involve McAllister Ranch.
In all likelihood,
you will appear on
behalf of your client to provide testimony and answer questions.
You will also contact individual council members to discuss the
owners’ position and the history of the project.
You will appear
in your private capacity as a representative of the property
owners,
not as a public official.
At a later time,
you would like to appear before the Local
Agency Formation Commission
(LAFCO)
on behalf of the owners of
McAllister Ranch.
ANALYSIS
Although you stated that you do not intend to appear before
the planning commission regarding McAllister Ranch or other
projects on which you are working, the following provides general
guidance regarding the conflict-of—interest provisions of the Act,
as it pertains to your office as a planning commissioner.
The purpose of the conflict-of—interest provisions of the Act
is to ensure that public officials, whether elected or appointed,
perform their duties in an impartial manner,
free from bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them.
(Section 81001(b).)
In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 of the Act provides:
No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in making
or in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence
a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.
As a member of the Bakersfield Planning Commission,
you are a
“public official” as defined in the Act.
(Section 82048.)
Thus,
you may not use your official position to influence a governmental
decision in which you know or have reason to know you have a
financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in
a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable


Our File No.
A-93-114
Page
3
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
Section 87103(c).
As an employee of DeWalt Corporation,
you have earned in
excess of $250.
Consequently, you may not participate in any
governmental decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable
2
material financial effect on your employer.
Moreover,
a public official is required to disqualify from
participation when there is a “nexus” between the purpose for
which the official receives income and the governmental decision
before the official’s agency.
A nexus exists if the official
receives income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be
achieved, defeated, aided,
or hindered by the governmental
decision.
(Regulation 18702.1(d).)
Regulation 18702.1(a)
(copy enclosed)
provides that the ef
fect of a governmental decision on an official’s economic
interests is deemed to be material and disqualification is
required if the source of income is directly before the official’s
agency,
or there is a nexus between the purpose for which the
official receives income and the decision to be made by the
official’s governmental agency.
(See Regulation 18702.1(b)
for a
definition of “directly involved.”)
In addition,
you must also disqualify from planning
commission decisions which will have an indirect material
financial effect on any of your financial interests pursuant to
Regulations 18702.2 and 18702.6.
Participation in governmental decisions has been interpreted
broadly in furtherance of the goals of the Act.
Thus,
where you
2
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(.Ii
re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)


Our File No.
A-93-114
Page
4
are disqualified with respect to planning commission decisions,
you are also prohibited from making contacts with,
appearing
before,
or otherwise attempting to influence,
any member, officer,
employee or consultant of your agency concerning the governmental
decision.
(Regulations 18700 and 18700.1).
However,
you are not
prohibited from preparing drawings or submissions of an
architectural,
engineering or similar nature to be used by a
client in connection with a proceeding,
and responding to
inquiries from planning commission staff concerning the processing
or evaluation of the drawings or submissions,
provided you have no
other direct oral or written contact with the agency.
(Regulation
18700.1(b) (4);
West Advice Letter,
No.
A—88—413.)
Decisions of Other Agencies
You stated that there is a sphere of influence amendment
before the Bakersfield City Council and you would like to
represent the owners of McAllister Ranch as an employee of DeWalt
Corporation.
You have asked to what extent you may participate in
the presentation of the project to the city in your private
capacity.
Specifically,
you would like to appear before the city
council to provide testimony and answer questions; you would also
like to contact individual council members to discuss the owners’
position and the history of the project.
In addition,
you expect that the sphere of influence issue
will ultimately come before the Local Agency Formation Commission
(LAFCO),
which is not a subdivision or commission of the city.
You would also like to appear before LAFCO as a representative of
the owners of McAllister Ranch.
Under the Act,
you may not use your official position to
influence a governmental decision.
Regulation 18700.1 provides:
(a)
With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an official’s agency or
an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of his or her agency,
the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts,
or
appears before,
or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the
agency.
Attempts to influence include,
but are not
limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official
on behalf of a business entity, client,
or
customer.
(Emphasis added.)
***


Our File No.
A-93--114
Page 5
(c)
With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an agency not covered by
subsection (a), the official is attempting to use
his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official acts or purports to act on
behalf of,
or as the representative of, his or her
agency to any member, officer,
employee or
consultant of an agency.
Such actions include, but
are not limited to the use of official stationery.
(Emphasis added.)
Pursuant to Regulation 18700.1(c), as long as you do not
purport to represent the planning commission,
you are not
restricted in representing your clients before the city council or
LAFCO in your private capacity.
I trust this answers your questions.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
By:
unsel,
Legal Division
JM:JS:ak
Enclosure

QUESTION: 1)
There is an application before the Bakersfield City
Council to amend the sphere of influence.
May you appear before
the city council in your capacity as an employee of the DeWalt
Corporation on behalf of your clients, the owners of McAllister
Ranch?
2)
May you appear before
LAFCO in your capacity as an
employee of the DeWalt corporation on behalf of your clients, the
owners of McAllister Ranch?

CONCLUSION: S
1 and
2)
Under the Act,
as long as you do not purport to
represent the planning commission,
you may represent your clients
in your private capacity before the city council and LAFCO.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission April 28, 1993 Jim Marino Special Project Coordinator DeWalt Corporation 2340 Niles Street Bakersfield, California 93306 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-93-114 Dear Mr. Marino: You have requested advice regarding your duties as a planning commissioner under the conflict—of—interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) 1 QUESTIONS 1) There is an application before the Bakersfield City Council to amend the sphere of influence. May you appear before the city council in your capacity as an employee of the DeWalt Corporation on behalf of your clients, the owners of McAllister Ranch? 2) May you appear before LAFCO in your capacity as an employee of the DeWalt corporation on behalf of your clients, the owners of McAllister Ranch? CONCLUS IONS 1 and 2) Under the Act, as long as you do not purport to represent the planning commission, you may represent your clients in your private capacity before the city council and LAFCO. FACTS You serve as a planning commissioner for the City of Bakersfield. You are employed as a special projects coordinator for the DeWalt Corporation, a civil engineering, surveying, 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000 et g. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. A-93-114 Page 2 planning and land development company in Kern County, California. You are a salaried employee, with no ownership interest in the company. The DeWalt Corporation represents the owners of McAllister Ranch, a master planned community, in the unincorporated area in the county of Kern. In the past twelve months, your employer has paid you approximately $20,000 for your work on the McAllister Ranch project. There is a possibility that you may acquire an ownership interest in the project in the future. An issue coming before the Bakersfield City Council regards amendment to the current sphere of influence line, which will involve McAllister Ranch. In all likelihood, you will appear on behalf of your client to provide testimony and answer questions. You will also contact individual council members to discuss the owners’ position and the history of the project. You will appear in your private capacity as a representative of the property owners, not as a public official. At a later time, you would like to appear before the Local Agency Formation Commission (LAFCO) on behalf of the owners of McAllister Ranch.
